com:Annotation.com:AnnotationText: presentation_title=WEO Projections; presentation_description=Real GDP growth (NGDP_RPCH) projections for aggregates and key economies

str:Dataflow.com:Name: World Economic Outlook (WEO)
str:Dataflow.com:Description: The World Economic Outlook (WEO) database is created during the biannual WEO exercise, which begins in January and June of each year and results in the April and September/October WEO publication. Selected series from the publication are available in a database format.

str:Codelist.com:Name: Security Classification
str:Codelist.com:Description: This codelist designates the level of sensitivity or confidentiality associated with data, dictating the protective measures required for its handling, storage, and transmission.

str:Code.com:Name: Public or Unrestricted
str:Code.com:Description: Information that has been published in full by the Fund and has been approved to be made available inside and outside the Fund without restriction is “Public” or “Unclassified.”

str:Codelist.com:Name: Department
str:Codelist.com:Description: This codelist identifies the internal departments or organizational units within an institution (e.g., the IMF) that are primarily responsible for the creation, maintenance, or dissemination of a specific data resource.

str:Codelist.com:Name: Organization
str:Codelist.com:Description: This codelist identifies and categorizes institutions relevant to economic, financial, and statistical data, providing standardized codes and corresponding names. It includes international organizations, central banks, and government institutions involved in data collection and dissemination.

str:Code.com:Name: International Monetary Fund (IMF)
str:Code.com:Description: https://www.imf.org/en/Home

str:Codelist.com:Name: Topic
str:Codelist.com:Description: This codelist provides a high-level categorization of the resource's subject matter, indicating the main area or domain to which the data pertains.

str:Code.com:Name: Demographic and social
str:Code.com:Description: Population demographics, social indicators, and labor market statistics.

str:Code.com:Name: Labor markets
str:Code.com:Description: The labor market, encompassing employment, unemployment, labor force participation, wages, and other labor-related indicators.

str:Code.com:Name: Labor force
str:Code.com:Description: The total number of people employed and unemployed who are willing and able to work. This provides insights into the size and composition of the workforce and is used to analyze labor market trends and calculate unemployment rates.

str:Code.com:Name: Unemployment
str:Code.com:Description: The number of people who are actively seeking employment but cannot find a job. This is used to calculate the unemployment rate, a key indicator of economic performance and social well-being.

str:Code.com:Name: Employment
str:Code.com:Description: The number of people currently employed, including information on the types of jobs, industries, and occupations.

str:Code.com:Name: Wages
str:Code.com:Description: The earnings of employees, including hourly wages, salaries, and other forms of compensation. This is used to analyze income inequality, measure labor costs, and assess the standard of living.

str:Code.com:Name: Population and demographics
str:Code.com:Description: The size, structure, and distribution of a population.

str:Code.com:Name: Population
str:Code.com:Description: The total number of people residing in a specific geographic area, such as a country, region, or city. This is essential for demographic analysis, resource allocation, and policy planning.

str:Code.com:Name: Demographic change
str:Code.com:Description: The changes in the size, composition, and distribution of a population over time. This includes information on birth rates, death rates, age structure, migration patterns, and other demographic factors.

str:Code.com:Name: Migration
str:Code.com:Description: The movement of people across geographical boundaries, including both internal and international migration.

str:Code.com:Name: Education
str:Code.com:Description: Education, encompassing enrollment rates, educational attainment, literacy levels, and educational expenditures.

str:Code.com:Name: Health
str:Code.com:Description: The health of a population.

str:Code.com:Name: Inclusion and gender
str:Code.com:Description: Gender, encompassing measures of equality, diversity, and social participation across different demographic groups.

str:Code.com:Name: Women
str:Code.com:Description: The social, economic, and political status of women, including indicators of gender equality, labor force participation, education, health, and political representation.

str:Code.com:Name: SME finance
str:Code.com:Description: The financing of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), including information on loan volumes, interest rates, and access to credit.

str:Code.com:Name: Technology
str:Code.com:Description: Technological innovation, adoption, and impact.

str:Code.com:Name: Fintech
str:Code.com:Description: Financial technology innovations, including digital payments, peer-to-peer lending, robo-advisors, and blockchain-based solutions.

str:Code.com:Name: Environment and climate
str:Code.com:Description: Environmental conditions, climate change, and natural resource use.

str:Code.com:Name: Climate
str:Code.com:Description: Climate patterns, trends, and impacts.

str:Code.com:Name: Climate change
str:Code.com:Description: Observed changes in temperature, precipitation, sea level, and other climate variables, as well as projections of future climate change under different scenarios.

str:Code.com:Name: Natural disasters
str:Code.com:Description: The occurrence, frequency, intensity, and impact of natural disasters, such as earthquakes, floods, droughts, storms, and wildfires.

str:Code.com:Name: Climate change adaptation
str:Code.com:Description: The strategies, measures, and investments aimed at reducing the vulnerability of natural and human systems to the impacts of climate change. This includes information on infrastructure resilience, early warning systems, and agricultural practices.

str:Code.com:Name: Climate change mitigation
str:Code.com:Description: The actions taken to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and enhance carbon sinks, such as transitioning to renewable energy, improving energy efficiency, and promoting sustainable land use practices.

str:Code.com:Name: Climate finance
str:Code.com:Description: The financial flows channeled towards climate change mitigation and adaptation projects and programs, including both public and private investments, as well as international climate finance mechanisms.

str:Code.com:Name: Climate risks
str:Code.com:Description: The potential adverse consequences of climate change for various sectors and systems, including physical risks (e.g., extreme weather events, sea-level rise), transition risks (e.g., policy changes, technological shifts), and liability risks (e.g., legal claims, reputational damage).

str:Code.com:Name: Greenhouse gas emissions
str:Code.com:Description: The amount of greenhouse gases, such as carbon dioxide, methane, and nitrous oxide, released into the atmosphere from various sources, including energy production, industry, agriculture, and transportation.

str:Code.com:Name: Climate and weather
str:Code.com:Description: The long-term patterns and average conditions of temperature, humidity, precipitation, wind, etc. in a specific region (climate), as well as the day-to-day state of the atmosphere (weather).

str:Code.com:Name: Transition to a low carbon economy
str:Code.com:Description: The global shift away from economic activities that rely heavily on fossil fuels (like coal, oil, and natural gas) and towards systems that generate minimal greenhouse gas emissions. This includes data on renewable energy adoption, energy efficiency improvements, and sustainable technology development.

str:Code.com:Name: Physical and transition risk indicators
str:Code.com:Description: Physical and transition risks related to climate change. Physical risks refer to the potential impact of climate change on physical assets, while transition risks are related to the shift to a low-carbon economy and include factors such as policy changes and technological innovations.

str:Code.com:Name: Environment
str:Code.com:Description: The natural environment and its interactions with human activities.

str:Code.com:Name: Economy and prices
str:Code.com:Description: Economic activity, prices, and national accounts.

str:Code.com:Name: National accounts
str:Code.com:Description: Describes and measures the economic activity of a country or region. It provides a comprehensive overview of the economy's structure, performance, and interactions between different sectors. This data is compiled within a standardized framework, the System of National Accounts (SNA).

str:Code.com:Name: Gross domestic product (GDP)
str:Code.com:Description: The total value of goods and services produced within a country's borders over a specific period.

str:Code.com:Name: National income
str:Code.com:Description: The total income earned by a country's residents, including wages, profits, rents, and interest.

str:Code.com:Name: Sector accounts
str:Code.com:Description: The economic activity of specific sectors within the economy, such as agriculture, manufacturing, construction, and services.

str:Code.com:Name: Industrial production
str:Code.com:Description: The output of industrial establishments, including manufacturing, mining, and utilities. This is used to track industrial activity, assess productivity, and monitor economic cycles.

str:Code.com:Name: Prices
str:Code.com:Description: Statistical information that captures the monetary values at which goods, services, assets, or other items are bought, sold, or exchanged in a market. This can encompass various aspects of prices.

str:Code.com:Name: Consumer prices
str:Code.com:Description: The average change over time in the prices paid by consumers for a basket of goods and services.

str:Code.com:Name: Producer prices
str:Code.com:Description: The average change over time in the selling prices received by domestic producers for their output.

str:Code.com:Name: Real estate prices
str:Code.com:Description: The prices of houses, apartments, land, and other real estate properties.

str:Code.com:Name: Commodity prices
str:Code.com:Description: The prices of raw materials and primary agricultural products, such as oil, gas, metals, grains, and livestock.

str:Code.com:Name: Export price indexes
str:Code.com:Description: The average change in prices of goods and services exported by a country.

str:Code.com:Name: Producer price indexes (PPI)
str:Code.com:Description: The average change in prices received by domestic producers for their output.

str:Code.com:Name: Import price indexes
str:Code.com:Description: The average change in prices of goods and services imported by a country.

str:Code.com:Name: Wholesale price indexes
str:Code.com:Description: The average change in prices of goods sold in bulk by wholesalers to retailers.

str:Code.com:Name: International trade
str:Code.com:Description: The exchange of goods and services between countries.

str:Code.com:Name: Trade in services
str:Code.com:Description: The value of intangible services (e.g., tourism, transportation, financial services) exchanged between countries.

str:Code.com:Name: Trade in goods
str:Code.com:Description: The value and volume of physical goods (e.g., merchandise, commodities) that are exported and imported.

str:Code.com:Name: Exports
str:Code.com:Description: The value and volume of goods and services sold to other countries.

str:Code.com:Name: Imports
str:Code.com:Description: The value and volume of goods and services purchased from other countries.

str:Code.com:Name: Production
str:Code.com:Description: The output of goods and services in various sectors of the economy.

str:Code.com:Name: Tourism
str:Code.com:Description: The number of tourists visiting a country and their spending.

str:Code.com:Name: External sector
str:Code.com:Description: International trade, balance of payments, and external debt.

str:Code.com:Name: Balance of payments
str:Code.com:Description: The recording of all economic transactions between residents of a country and the rest of the world over a specific period, usually a year. This includes transactions related to goods, services, income, and financial assets.

str:Code.com:Name: Current account
str:Code.com:Description: A country's transactions with the rest of the world, including the balance of trade in goods and services, net income from abroad, and net current transfers.

str:Code.com:Name: Capital account
str:Code.com:Description: A country's transactions related to capital transfers and the acquisition and disposal of non-produced, non-financial assets.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial account
str:Code.com:Description: A country's transactions related to financial assets and liabilities, including direct investment, portfolio investment, and other investments.

str:Code.com:Name: Foreign portfolio investment (FPI)
str:Code.com:Description: Cross-border investments in financial assets, such as stocks, bonds, and other securities, where investors do not have a controlling stake in the underlying companies.

str:Code.com:Name: Foreign direct investment (FDI)
str:Code.com:Description: Cross-border investments in which an investor establishes a lasting interest in an enterprise operating in another country. This includes data on mergers and acquisitions, greenfield investments, and the establishment of subsidiaries or branches.

str:Code.com:Name: External position
str:Code.com:Description: A country's overall financial position with respect to the rest of the world at a specific point in time. It combines data from the balance of payments and international investment position (IIP) to provide a snapshot of a country's external assets, liabilities, and net worth.

str:Code.com:Name: International investment position (IIP)
str:Code.com:Description: A country's stock of external financial assets and liabilities at a specific point in time, including direct investment, portfolio investment, other investments, and reserve assets.

str:Code.com:Name: External debt
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of money owed by a country's government, businesses, and individuals to foreign creditors.

str:Code.com:Name: Fiscal sector
str:Code.com:Description: Government revenue, expenditure, and debt.

str:Code.com:Name: Revenue administration
str:Code.com:Description: The collection of government revenue, primarily through taxes, fees, and other levies.

str:Code.com:Name: Expenditure
str:Code.com:Description: Government spending on goods, services, and transfer payments, including details on budget allocations, actual expenditures, and program outcomes.

str:Code.com:Name: Public debt
str:Code.com:Description: The total outstanding debt owed by a government, encompassing both domestic and external debt, as well as information on the types of debt instruments, interest rates, maturity profiles, and debt-to-GDP ratios.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial and monetary sector
str:Code.com:Description: Financial institutions, monetary aggregates, and interest rates.

str:Code.com:Name: Foreign exchange
str:Code.com:Description: The exchange rates between different currencies, including spot rates, forward rates, and exchange rate regimes, as well as information on foreign exchange reserves, capital flows, and international trade.

str:Code.com:Name: International reserves
str:Code.com:Description: A country's holdings of external assets, such as foreign currencies, gold, and Special Drawing Rights (SDRs), held by the central bank to meet external financial obligations and stabilize exchange rates.

str:Code.com:Name: Exchange rates
str:Code.com:Description: The price of one currency in terms of another, reflecting the value of a currency relative to other currencies. This includes spot exchange rates, forward rates, and exchange rate regimes.

str:Code.com:Name: Central banks
str:Code.com:Description: The activities of central banks, such as monetary policy decisions, interest rate setting, open market operations, reserve management, and financial stability measures.

str:Code.com:Name: Banking
str:Code.com:Description: The activities and performance of the banking sector, including assets, liabilities, capital, deposits, loans, and profitability. This is used to monitor the health and stability of the banking system.

str:Code.com:Name: Central bank balance sheet
str:Code.com:Description: The assets and liabilities of the central bank, including foreign reserves, government securities, and loans to commercial banks. This provides insights into the central bank's monetary policy operations and its role in the economy.

str:Code.com:Name: Central bank credit
str:Code.com:Description: The credit extended by the central bank to the banking sector and the government, including discount window lending, repurchase agreements, and emergency lending facilities.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial markets, services and instruments
str:Code.com:Description: The trading and pricing of financial assets, such as stocks, bonds, derivatives, and currencies, as well as information on financial institutions, market infrastructure, and regulatory frameworks.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial instruments
str:Code.com:Description: Various financial instruments, such as stocks, bonds, derivatives, and other securities, including their issuance, trading volumes, prices, and returns.

str:Code.com:Name: Loans
str:Code.com:Description: The amount of credit extended by financial institutions to households and businesses, including information on loan types, interest rates, maturity profiles, and credit quality.

str:Code.com:Name: Insurance
str:Code.com:Description: The insurance sector, including premiums, claims, assets, liabilities, and profitability of insurance companies.

str:Code.com:Name: Interest rates
str:Code.com:Description: The cost of borrowing and the return on savings, typically expressed as a percentage of the principal amount. This includes data on various interest rate benchmarks, such as the policy rate set by the central bank, interbank rates, and lending rates for different types of borrowers.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial access
str:Code.com:Description: The availability and affordability of financial services, such as bank accounts, credit, insurance, and payment systems, for individuals and businesses.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial sector stability
str:Code.com:Description: The resilience of the financial system to shocks and its ability to withstand financial stress, including indicators of asset quality, capital adequacy, liquidity, profitability, and risk management practices of financial institutions.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial stability assessment
str:Code.com:Description: The evaluation of the resilience of the financial system to shocks and its ability to withstand financial stress. This includes indicators of asset quality, capital adequacy, liquidity, profitability, and risk management practices of financial institutions.

str:Code.com:Name: Solvency
str:Code.com:Description: The ability of a financial institution or a government to meet its long-term financial obligations, including the payment of interest and principal on debt.

str:Code.com:Name: Stress testing
str:Code.com:Description: The simulation of adverse economic scenarios to assess the resilience of financial institutions and the broader financial system to potential shocks.

str:Code.com:Name: Systemic risk
str:Code.com:Description: The risk of a widespread disruption to the financial system that could have severe consequences for the broader economy. This includes indicators of interconnectedness, leverage, and concentration in the financial system.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial sector risk
str:Code.com:Description: The specific risks faced by different types of financial institutions, such as credit risk, market risk, operational risk, and liquidity risk.

str:Code.com:Name: Securities
str:Code.com:Description: Financial instruments that represent ownership in a company (stocks) or debt obligations (bonds). This includes information on issuance, trading volumes, prices, and returns.

str:Code.com:Name: Deposits
str:Code.com:Description: The funds held by individuals and businesses in bank accounts, including demand deposits, time deposits, and savings accounts.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial derivatives
str:Code.com:Description: Financial contracts whose value is derived from an underlying asset, such as stocks, bonds, commodities, or currencies.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial institutions
str:Code.com:Description: The financial activities and performance of banks, insurance companies, pension funds, investment funds, and other financial intermediaries, including balance sheets, income statements, and risk exposures.

str:Code.com:Name: Commercial banks
str:Code.com:Description: The financial activities and performance of commercial banks, which are the primary providers of credit and deposit services to households and businesses. This includes balance sheets, income statements, and risk exposures.

str:Code.com:Name: Nonbank financial institutions
str:Code.com:Description: The activities and performance of financial institutions that are not banks, such as insurance companies, pension funds, investment funds, and other financial intermediaries.

str:Code.com:Name: Monetary policy
str:Code.com:Description: The actions taken by central banks to influence the availability and cost of money in the economy, such as interest rate changes, quantitative easing, and other monetary policy tools.

str:Code.com:Name: Reserve requirements
str:Code.com:Description: The minimum amount of reserves that banks are required to hold against their deposits, as mandated by the central bank.

str:Code.com:Name: Money
str:Code.com:Description: The various forms of money in circulation, including currency, demand deposits, and other liquid assets.

str:Code.com:Name: Monetary aggregates
str:Code.com:Description: The total stock of money in circulation, including currency in circulation, demand deposits, and other liquid assets. Monetary aggregates are used as indicators of economic activity and to gauge the impact of monetary policy.

str:Code.com:Name: Monetary base
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of currency in circulation and reserves held by commercial banks at the central bank. The monetary base is a key component of the money supply and is directly controlled by the central bank.

str:Code.com:Name: Credit aggregates
str:Code.com:Description: The total amount of credit outstanding in the economy, including loans to households, businesses, and the government.

str:Code.com:Name: Macrofinancial analysis
str:Code.com:Description: The assessment of the overall health and stability of a country's financial system, including its interactions with the broader economy. This encompasses indicators of credit growth, asset prices, capital flows, and other financial variables that can affect economic stability.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial cycles
str:Code.com:Description: The fluctuations in financial variables over time, such as credit growth, asset prices, and leverage.

str:Code.com:Name: Macrofinancial linkages
str:Code.com:Description: The interconnections between the financial sector and the broader economy, including the transmission channels through which financial shocks can affect economic activity and vice versa.

str:Code.com:Name: Macroprudential policy
str:Code.com:Description: Policies and regulations designed to mitigate systemic risks in the financial system, such as capital requirements, leverage ratios, and borrower-based measures.

str:Code.com:Name: Macroprudential policy instruments
str:Code.com:Description: The specific tools used by policymakers to mitigate systemic risks in the financial system, such as capital buffers, leverage ratios, and borrower-based measures.

str:Code.com:Name: Macroprudential analysis
str:Code.com:Description: The assessment of systemic risks and vulnerabilities in the financial system, using both quantitative and qualitative methods.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial supervision and regulation
str:Code.com:Description: The oversight and enforcement activities of regulatory bodies overseeing financial institutions and markets. This includes information on regulatory frameworks, supervisory practices, and enforcement actions.

str:Code.com:Name: Bank regulation
str:Code.com:Description: The rules and regulations governing the operations of banks, including capital requirements, liquidity requirements, and risk management standards.

str:Code.com:Name: Bank risk management
str:Code.com:Description: The practices and procedures used by banks to identify, assess, and mitigate risks, such as credit risk, market risk, operational risk, and liquidity risk.

str:Code.com:Name: Nonbank financial institution supervision
str:Code.com:Description: The oversight and regulation of nonbank financial institutions, such as insurance companies, pension funds, and investment funds.

str:Code.com:Name: Asset and liability management (ALM)
str:Code.com:Description: The strategies and practices employed by financial institutions to manage their assets and liabilities, including interest rate risk, liquidity risk, and credit risk. This is used to assess the effectiveness of ALM in maintaining financial soundness.

str:Code.com:Name: Liability risk measures
str:Code.com:Description: Various measures used to assess the riskiness of an institution's liabilities, such as the maturity mismatch between assets and liabilities, the volatility of funding sources, and the potential for runs on deposits.

str:Code.com:Name: Economic forecasts and analysis
str:Code.com:Description: Economic forecasts, policy analysis, and other economic research.

str:Code.com:Name: Economic forecasting
str:Code.com:Description: Economic forecasts for various countries and regions.

str:Code.com:Name: Fund accounts
str:Code.com:Description: The IMF's financial accounts and resources.

str:Code.com:Name: IMF Financial data
str:Code.com:Description: The IMF's financial operations and lending activities.

str:Code.com:Name: IMF Surveillance reports
str:Code.com:Description: Reports on the IMF's surveillance of member countries' economic and financial policies.

str:Code.com:Name: Working papers datasets
str:Code.com:Description: Datasets relating to working papers, which are typically preliminary research papers that present new findings, methodologies, or analysis for academic or policy-related discussion.

str:Code.com:Name: International Financial Statistics (IFS)
str:Code.com:Description: Datasets underlying the International Financial Statistics, one of the Fund’s principal statistical compilations. Available since 1948, it has broad geographic and conceptual coverage.

str:Code.com:Name: World Economic Outlook (WEO)
str:Code.com:Description: Datasets related to the World Economic Outlook and Regional Economic Outlooks

str:Code.com:Name: Third-Party Data
str:Code.com:Description: Licensed economic and financial data from commercial and non-profit providers, subject to strict copyright terms. External data.

str:Codelist.com:Name: Language
str:Codelist.com:Description: This codelist identifies the natural languages in which a data resource is available or presented, using standardized codes based on the ISO 639-1 standard.

str:Codelist.com:Name: Access and Sharing Level
str:Codelist.com:Description: This codelist defines the allowable levels of access and sharing for a particular data resource, specifying who can view, use, or distribute the data and under what conditions.

str:Code.com:Name: Public (Unrestricted)
str:Code.com:Description: Share this data with the public without restrictions.

str:ConceptScheme.com:Name: Master Domain Concepts
str:ConceptScheme.com:Description: Standardized definitions for data elements specific to particular domains or subject areas. This includes concepts and attributes related to macroeconomics, finance, socio-demographics, and other specialized fields.

str:Concept.com:Name: Accounting Entry
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification that identifies whether a transaction represents a use of resources, a source of funds, an asset, a liability, or a change in equity.

str:Concept.com:Name: Commodity
str:Concept.com:Description: Comprehensive list of all the different commodity codes used in IMF.

str:Concept.com:Name: Sector
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification of economic entities based on their primary function and economic behavior, such as households, corporations, government, or non-profit institutions.

str:Concept.com:Name: Counterpart Sector
str:Concept.com:Description: In the context of a specific transaction or economic flow, the counterpart sector refers to the institutional sector on the opposite side of the transaction.

str:Concept.com:Name: Exchange Rate
str:Concept.com:Description: Represents a set of codes identifying the exchange rate between two currencies. An exchange rate indicates how much of one currency is needed to buy one unit of another currency. It is derived by comparing the value of one currency to another, often influenced by economic factors like interest rates, inflation, and government policy. This codelist includes various exchange rate calculations, such as period averages, pre-Euro rates, and those involving Special Drawing Rights (SDRs).

str:Concept.com:Name: Flow or stock entry
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification that indicates whether a data point represents a flow (a quantity measured over a period of time) or a stock (a quantity measured at a specific point in time).

str:Concept.com:Name: Functional category
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification used to categorize investments based on their purpose or function, such as equity investments, debt investments, or other investments.

str:Concept.com:Name: GFS Stocks, Transactions, Other Flows
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification of data items used in the Government Finance Statistics (GFS) framework, distinguishing between stocks (levels at a point in time), transactions (changes over a period), and other flows.

str:Concept.com:Name: Index type
str:Concept.com:Description: Type of index prices.

str:Concept.com:Name: Interest Rate types, Terms and Conditions
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification of interest rates based on their type (fixed or floating), terms (maturity), and other conditions (e.g., collateralization, reference rates).

str:Concept.com:Name: Instrument and Assets Classification
str:Concept.com:Description: Because of the symmetry of financial assets and liabilities, the term ‘instrument’ is used to relate to both the asset and the liability aspect of financial transactions, other flows and financial balance sheets. (Source: Handbook of National Accounting, 2013)

str:Concept.com:Name: International Accounts Item
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification of transactions in the balance of payments, such as trade in goods, services, income, and financial flows.

str:Concept.com:Name: Macroeconomic and Financial Accounts
str:Concept.com:Description: A framework for organizing macroeconomic and financial data into a set of integrated accounts and tables that provide a comprehensive overview of an economy.

str:Concept.com:Name: Maturity of Financial Instrument
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification of financial instruments based on their remaining time to maturity or expiration.

str:Concept.com:Name: Monetary and Financial Instruments
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification of financial instruments used in monetary and financial statistics, including currency, deposits, loans, securities, and derivatives.

str:Concept.com:Name: Stocks, Transactions, Other Flows
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification of data items used in the System of National Accounts (SNA) framework, distinguishing between stocks (levels at a point in time), transactions (changes over a period), and other flows.

str:Concept.com:Name: Prices
str:Concept.com:Description: Represents different types of prices or price adjustments, including those that have been adjusted for inflation (real or constant prices) and those that have not (nominal or current prices). It also includes various methods for adjusting prices for inflation, such as using deflators or converting to a constant base period.

str:Concept.com:Name: Statistical Measures
str:Concept.com:Description: Describe characteristics of a group of data. They summarize the distribution, central tendency, or spread of multiple values.

str:Concept.com:Name: Trade flow
str:Concept.com:Description: Data related to the movement of goods and services between countries, including exports and imports.

str:Concept.com:Name: Valuation
str:Concept.com:Description: Valuation refers to the rules and conventions used for recording monetary flows and stocks in macroeconomic statistics. Standard national accounts valuations include the basic price (what the seller receives) and the purchaser's price (what the purchaser pays). The purchaser's price is the basic price, plus taxes less subsidies on products, plus invoiced transportation and insurance services, plus distribution margin. International trade in goods considers the free on board (fob) price and cost-insurance-freight price, among others. Other valuation types include prices used in financial markets (e.g., buy and sell prices).

str:Concept.com:Name: Social Concepts
str:Concept.com:Description: A broad category encompassing various social indicators, such as tourism and labor concepts.

str:Concept.com:Name: COFOG
str:Concept.com:Description: The Classification of the Functions of Government, a standardized system for classifying government expenditures by purpose.

str:Concept.com:Name: Expenditure Category
str:Concept.com:Description: The Classification of Individual Consumption According to Purpose (COICOP) 1999 is a standardized classification system developed by the United Nations Statistics Division to categorize household consumption expenditures by purpose. It includes 14 divisions, 47 groups, and 117 classes.

str:Concept.com:Name: Expenditure Category (COICOP 2018)
str:Concept.com:Description: The Classification of Individual Consumption According to Purpose (COICOP) 2018 is an updated and expanded version of the COICOP classification system, designed to better reflect the evolving consumption patterns of the 21st century. It includes 14 divisions, 50 groups, 153 classes, and 449 subclasses.

str:Concept.com:Name: Country
str:Concept.com:Description: The country or region for which the data or statistics in a resource are collected or reported.

str:Concept.com:Name: Indicator
str:Concept.com:Description: A variable that sheds light on some phenomenon or situation.

str:Concept.com:Name: Latest Actual Annual Data
str:Concept.com:Description: This indicates that latest annual period for which official statistics are available from the authorities. Data following this period are normal staff estimates.

str:Concept.com:Name: Fiscal Sector: Instruments included in gross and net debt

str:ConceptScheme.com:Name: Master Metadata Concepts
str:ConceptScheme.com:Description: Standardized definitions for common data elements used across multiple datasets. These elements include attributes like identification and provenance, access controls, methodology and other general descriptive characteristics.

str:Concept.com:Name: DOI
str:Concept.com:Description: A public identifier (unique alphanumeric string) assigned to a digital object, typically a publication or dataset, to provide a persistent link to its location on the internet.Users use it for citing the resource.

str:Concept.com:Name: Series Name
str:Concept.com:Description: A label given to a specific series. This name helps to distinguish it from other series data and provides context for what the data represents.

str:Concept.com:Name: Author
str:Concept.com:Description: The person or organization responsible for resource’s substantive and intellectual content.

str:Concept.com:Name: Publisher
str:Concept.com:Description: The organization or institution responsible for making a resource available to the public or a specific audience. For datasets, the publisher is responsible for preparing and releasing the dataset for public or private use. This includes ensuring the dataset's quality, accuracy, and accessibility.

str:Concept.com:Name: Department
str:Concept.com:Description: The specific division or unit within an organization (e.g., the IMF) that is ultimately accountable for the creation, maintenance, and dissemination of a resource.

str:Concept.com:Name: Creator
str:Concept.com:Description: The creator is the entity primarily responsible for creating the resource. Institution responsible for producing official statistics.

str:Concept.com:Name: Data Owner
str:Concept.com:Description: Institution that holds the legal ownership and accountability for the data, ensuring it aligns with national or international policies.

str:Concept.com:Name: Data Steward
str:Concept.com:Description: Institution that ensures the quality and metadata of the data, and that it adheres to relevant standards.

str:Concept.com:Name: Publication Date
str:Concept.com:Description: The date when a resource was formally issued or made available to the public or a specific audience. This applies to datasets that have major releases, but the data may have minor updates throughout the year. In most cases where the data is regularly updated, the publication date is the same as the update date.

str:Concept.com:Name: Update Date
str:Concept.com:Description: The most recent date on which a resource was modified or updated, including changes to data or metadata. For a dataset, the update date is the time when the data within a dataset was last modified, or the metadata was modified.

str:Concept.com:Name: Source Organization
str:Concept.com:Description: The entity responsible for producing or collecting the original data used in a resource. This could be a government agency, a research institution, a private company, or any other entity that generates data. When citing or referencing information, it is important to identify the source organization to ensure credibility and accuracy.

str:Concept.com:Name: Short Source Citation
str:Concept.com:Description: A brief reference to the source of data or information used in a resource, typically including the author, title, and year of publication.

str:Concept.com:Name: Full Source Citation
str:Concept.com:Description: A complete reference to the source of data or information used in a resource, providing all necessary details to locate and verify the original source.

str:Concept.com:Name: Suggested Citation
str:Concept.com:Description: A recommended format for citing a resource in academic or professional works, ensuring proper attribution and acknowledgement of the source.

str:Concept.com:Name: Full Description
str:Concept.com:Description: A comprehensive description of the dataset, encompassing its content, scope, underlying concepts, coverage, classifications, and statistical techniques employed in its creation and analysis. This may include references to detailed methodological documents or papers that provide further elaboration on these aspects.

str:Concept.com:Name: Topic
str:Concept.com:Description: A topic of the resource. A high-level categorization of the resource's subject matter, indicating the main area or domain to which the data pertains.

str:Concept.com:Name: Topic Dataset
str:Concept.com:Description: A topic of the dataset. A high-level categorization of the dataset's subject matter, indicating the main area or domain to which the data pertains.

str:Concept.com:Name: Keywords
str:Concept.com:Description: A set of words or phrases that describe the content and subject matter of a resource.

str:Concept.com:Name: Keywords Dataset
str:Concept.com:Description: A set of words or phrases that describe the content and subject matter of a dataset.

str:Concept.com:Name: Key Indicator
str:Concept.com:Description: A flag or indicator that identifies a data element or indicator as being of particular importance or significance for a specific analysis or purpose.

str:Concept.com:Name: Language
str:Concept.com:Description: It refers to the language of the intellectual content of the resource. It can be applied to any type of resource, including documents, datasets, images, audio recordings, etc.

str:Concept.com:Name: Temporal Coverage
str:Concept.com:Description: This field represents the temporal coverage of the data, expressed using the ISO 8601 standard. The data spans the time interval from 1997 to 2009 (inclusive), indicated as 1997/2009.

str:Concept.com:Name: Frequency Coverage
str:Concept.com:Description: Frequency Coverage in a dataset like annual or monthly refers to the range of time periods for which data is available.

str:Concept.com:Name: Geographical Coverage
str:Concept.com:Description: This field specifies the spatial extent of the data, detailing the geographic areas or regions included in the dataset. It may be represented by countries, continents, coordinates, or specific boundaries such as cities, regions, or global coverage.

str:Concept.com:Name: Methodology
str:Concept.com:Description: Reference to the conceptual statistical framework and underlying statistical methodology used for the dataset (e.g. country specific).

str:Concept.com:Name: Methodology Notes
str:Concept.com:Description: Additional details or explanations regarding the methodology used for a specific dataset or indicator. These notes outline the conceptual frameworks and statistical methods used to collect, process, and analyze the data, and can vary in level of detail depending on the data's complexity. For example: a) At the country level, methodology notes describe the specific methods used by each country to calculate particular indicators. b) At the time series level, methodology notes provide a detailed explanation of how an indicator is generated for a specific country.

str:Concept.com:Name: Contact Point
str:Concept.com:Description: The primary individual or group responsible for handling inquiries and providing information about a resource. For datasets published on the IMF data portal, the point of contact shown to public users is standardized as a common help email address.

str:Concept.com:Name: Security Classification
str:Concept.com:Description: A data classification assigned to data based on its sensitivity, value, and the potential impact of unauthorized access, disclosure, alteration, or destruction, resulting in the assignment of a tag that designates the data's sensitivity level. This attribute is assigned at lower levels of data when a dataset contains a mix of data access levels, with some series designated as public and others as confidential.

str:Concept.com:Name: Access and Sharing Level
str:Concept.com:Description: A classification that specifies the extent to which a resource can be shared with others, including restrictions on access, use, and distribution. It specifies who can access the data (e.g., public, specific groups, authorized personnel) and what actions they can take with it. The appropriate sharing level depends on the sensitivity and intended use of the data, ranging from open access for public data to restricted access for confidential information. This attribute is assigned at lower levels of data when a dataset contains a mix of data access levels, with some series designated as public and others as restricted.

str:Concept.com:Name: Access and Sharing Notes
str:Concept.com:Description: Additional notes involves documenting any нюances or complexities beyond the standard access and sharing levels.

str:Concept.com:Name: Access Instructions
str:Concept.com:Description: Detailed guidance on how to obtain or access a resource, particularly relevant for datasets.

str:Concept.com:Name: Usage Rights Notes
str:Concept.com:Description: Additional notes or explanations regarding the licensing terms for the source data used in a dataset.

str:Concept.com:Name: License
str:Concept.com:Description: Information on the licence for distribution / legal agreement that defines the terms under which a resource can be used, shared, or distributed.

str:Concept.com:Name: URL
str:Concept.com:Description: Direct link to the dataset on the external source's platform.

str:Concept.com:Name: Standard Classification Type
str:Concept.com:Description: Identifies the standard classifications used for the data.

str:ConceptScheme.com:Name: SDMX Concept Scheme for Concept Roles
str:ConceptScheme.com:Description: A concept role gives a particular context to a concept for easy and systematic interpretation by machine processing and visualization tools. For example, the concepts REPORTING_AREA and COUNTERPART_AREA are different concepts but they are both geographical characteristics, therefore they can be associated with the same concept role ID: “GEO”. This allows visualization systems to interpret these concepts as geographical data in order to generate maps. For any question, comment or correction, feel free to contact the SDMX Statistical Working Group (SWG) at the following address: swg@sdmx.org.

str:Concept.com:Name: Geographical
str:Concept.com:Description: Geographic area to which the measured statistical phenomenon relates.

str:Concept.com:Name: Flag
str:Concept.com:Description: Coded attribute in a data set that represents qualitative information for the cell or partial key (e.g. series) value.

str:Concept.com:Name: Frequency
str:Concept.com:Description: Time interval at which the source data are collected.

str:Concept.com:Name: Entity
str:Concept.com:Description: Describes the subject of the data set (e.g. a country).

str:Concept.com:Name: Comment
str:Concept.com:Description: Descriptive text which can be attached to data or metadata.

str:Concept.com:Name: Variable
str:Concept.com:Description: Characteristic of a unit being observed that may assume more than one of a set of values to which a numerical measure or a category from a classification can be assigned.

str:Concept.com:Name: Statistical operation
str:Concept.com:Description: Signifies statistical operations have been done on the observations.

str:ConceptScheme.com:Name: Master Data Concepts
str:ConceptScheme.com:Description: Standardized concepts and definitions for describing data elements across datasets, covering cross-cutting data attributes such as temporal information, country, unit of measure, transformation.

str:Concept.com:Name: Comments
str:Concept.com:Description: Additional notes, explanations, or remarks regarding the data, methodology, or other aspects of an observation.

str:Concept.com:Name: Observation Status
str:Concept.com:Description: Indicates the status of an observation within a dataset, such as whether it is a provisional value, an estimated value, a final figure, or a value where a time series break occurs. (Source: SDMX)

str:Concept.com:Name: Derivation Type
str:Concept.com:Description: Identifies the specific method used to derive a new data element or indicator from source data.

str:Concept.com:Name: IMF Data Overlap
str:Concept.com:Description: Indicates the potential for overlap within IMF data.

str:Concept.com:Name: Confidentiality Status
str:Concept.com:Description: This classification provides information about the sensitivity and confidentiality status of the data. (Source:SDMX)

str:Concept.com:Name: Reporting Period Type
str:Concept.com:Description: It is used to identify and distinguish between different types of time periods in economic and financial reporting. The specific start and end dates for a fiscal year (FY) can vary depending on the country, organization, or industry. When reporting data using this codelist, it is essential to specify the exact start and end dates of the fiscal year being referenced.

str:Concept.com:Name: Reference Period
str:Concept.com:Description: A time interval defined by a start date and an end date, used to constrain and specify the timeframe for reporting data.

str:Concept.com:Name: Base year
str:Concept.com:Description: The reference year used for comparison in the calculation of index numbers or other statistical measures. Changes in values over time are typically expressed as percentage deviations from the base year.

str:Concept.com:Name: Calendar year
str:Concept.com:Description: A period of twelve consecutive months starting on January 1st and ending on December 31st, as per the Gregorian calendar.

str:Concept.com:Name: Fiscal year
str:Concept.com:Description: A twelve-month period used by governments and businesses for accounting and financial reporting purposes. The start and end dates of a fiscal year can vary depending on the organization and country but often differ from the calendar year.

str:Concept.com:Name: Country
str:Concept.com:Description: The country or region for which the data or statistics in a resource are collected or reported.

str:Concept.com:Name: Counterpart Country
str:Concept.com:Description: The country or region with which a transaction or interaction occurs in the context of international statistics or data.

str:Concept.com:Name: Unit of Measure
str:Concept.com:Description: The standard unit used to quantify a particular data element or indicator.I It encompasses various units, including currency, physical units, percentages, and other standardized measures.

str:Concept.com:Name: Currency
str:Concept.com:Description: Denominated in Currency specifies the currency in which a financial instrument is valued and settled.

str:Concept.com:Name: Transformation
str:Concept.com:Description: Represents a set of codes identifying the specific transformations or calculations applied to source data to derive new data elements or indicators. A transformation involves a computation with one or more input values (either base variables obtained by observation or derived variables from prior transformations) that results in a single output value (the value of a new derived variable).

str:Concept.com:Name: Seasonal Adjustment
str:Concept.com:Description: Specific adjustment for the time series (e.g. seasonally adjusted).

str:Concept.com:Name: Gender
str:Concept.com:Description: Gender identity refers to each person’s internal and individual experience of gender. One's gender identity can be the same or different from their sex assigned at birth. (Source: UNECE 2023 Gender and salutation code lists)

str:Concept.com:Name: Sex
str:Concept.com:Description: An individual’s sex may be defined by one’s assigned sex at birth and/or one’s biological attributes which includes a combination of hormones, chromosomes, physiology, and anatomy. It includes both binary options as well as X and intersex. (Source: UNECE 2023 Gender and salutation code lists)

str:Concept.com:Name: Civil (or Marital) Status
str:Concept.com:Description: The legal status of an individual regarding marriage, such as single, married, widowed, or divorced.

str:Concept.com:Name: Country
str:Concept.com:Description: The country or region for which the data or statistics in a resource are collected or reported.

str:Concept.com:Name: Indicator
str:Concept.com:Description: A variable that sheds light on some phenomenon or situation.

str:Concept.com:Name: Latest Actual Annual Data
str:Concept.com:Description: This indicates that latest annual period for which official statistics are available from the authorities. Data following this period are normal staff estimates.

str:Concept.com:Name: Fiscal Sector: Instruments included in gross and net debt
